title: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose
summary: Argue that deliberate forgetting is a discipline of attention, not a failure of memory.
topic: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose
prompt: Write a 600–800-word essay arguing that there is a skilled, voluntary kind of forgetting — distinct from repression and distinct from carelessness — that should be recognised as a discipline. Use international references (Kyoto monasteries, Buenos Aires divorce courts, Berlin archives). Use inversion, a cleft, complex concession. No filler.
hintStarters: There is a temptation to treat forgetting as something that simply happens to us…
What is striking about deliberate forgetting is how easily it is confused with…
Granted, some things should never be forgotten, and we know which…
Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release can we…
sampleText:
There is a temptation to treat forgetting as something that simply happens to us — a leak in the bucket, the cost of getting older, the price the mind quietly pays for sleep. Most of our vocabulary about memory assumes this. We praise total recall and apologise for the lapse. Yet anyone who has watched a senior nurse in a Berlin ward leave a difficult shift behind before she opens her front door, or a judge in Buenos Aires put down a custody file at the end of a long Friday, knows that forgetting also exists in a second mode. It is willed, practised, and ethically loaded. It deserves a better name than absent-mindedness.

What is striking about deliberate forgetting is how easily it is confused with the bad neighbours on either side of it. On one side stands repression — the refusal to let something be known at all, which exacts its costs in the body and in the relationships of the person doing it. On the other side stands carelessness — the lazy refusal to encode a name, a debt, a promise, because it would cost effort to hold. Disciplined forgetting is neither. It is the cultivated capacity to receive an event fully, register it, file it, and then withdraw attention from it on purpose, so that the next event can be received with the same fullness.

The monastic traditions of Kyoto have a long, unsentimental literature on this. The point of certain meditations is not to erase memory but to loosen its grip — to allow what has happened to be present without demanding rehearsal. There is a similar discipline, less spiritualised, in the way a good emergency-room physician in São Paulo decides not to carry the third trauma of the night into the fourth. To call this 'forgetting' sounds, at first, almost rude. Yet the alternative — rehearsing every grief into the next encounter — does not honour the past; it just contaminates the present.

Granted, some things should never be forgotten, and we know which. The archives in Berlin and Buenos Aires were built precisely to hold the kind of memory that no individual mind is asked to carry alone. What I am defending is not the dilution of public memory but the existence, alongside it, of a private discipline that recognises the difference between what one is obliged to remember and what one is permitted, with care, to release. Without that distinction, the demand 'never forget' begins to corrode the very people we are asking to remember.

It is precisely the difference between consigning and erasing that matters here. To consign something is to send it somewhere — into a journal entry, into a conversation with a therapist or a friend, into the institutional archive — and to know that one need not hold it personally in active recall. To erase, by contrast, is to pretend it never happened. Disciplined forgetting consigns; it does not erase. Done well, it has the look, from outside, of grace — and the look, from inside, of a craft.

Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release can we ask the right questions of the people we admire for their composure. Are they composed because the event has been adequately consigned, or because it has been violently buried? The first is honourable; the second exacts a tax that someone will eventually pay. There is, then, something almost technical about asking a colleague after a hard week, 'where did you put it?' — meaning the meeting, the diagnosis, the loss. The question presumes that 'putting it somewhere' is a real and learnable move.

What would be lost if we admitted forgetting on purpose into the moral vocabulary of grown-up life? Very little, one might argue, and a great deal might be gained. A handful of grievances could be permitted to fade. A few archived sorrows could be left to the archives. And the attention freed by this small monastic discipline could be returned, intact, to the next person standing in front of us — which is, after all, the only person to whom such attention is owed.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only by …'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release can we ask the right questions…' đảo trợ động từ 'can' lên trước chủ ngữ 'we' sau cụm hạn định 'Only by + V-ing'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release can we ask the right questions…' inverts the modal 'can' before the subject 'we' after the restrictive 'Only by + V-ing' opener.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft for sharpening a distinction
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is precisely the difference between consigning and erasing that matters here.' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is … that …' để đẩy sự đối lập then chốt lên đầu, ép người đọc tập trung đúng vào điểm phân biệt.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'It is precisely the difference between consigning and erasing that matters here.' uses the cleft 'It is … that …' to front the crucial contrast, forcing the reader to focus on the right distinction.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có khuynh hướng coi sự quên như một điều chỉ xảy đến với ta — một lỗ rò trong xô, cái giá của tuổi tác, cái giá tâm trí âm thầm trả cho giấc ngủ. Phần lớn từ ngữ về kí ức đều giả định như vậy. Ta khen sự nhớ trọn vẹn và xin lỗi vì sự lãng quên. Nhưng bất kì ai đã nhìn một y tá trưởng ở một khoa bệnh Berlin bỏ lại ca trực khó khăn trước khi bà mở cửa nhà, hay một thẩm phán ở Buenos Aires đặt xuống một hồ sơ quyền nuôi con vào cuối một chiều thứ Sáu dài, đều biết rằng sự quên còn tồn tại ở một dạng thứ hai. Nó có chủ ý, được luyện tập, và mang trọng lượng đạo đức.

Điều đáng chú ý ở sự quên có chủ đích là nó dễ bị lẫn với hai người láng giềng xấu ở hai bên. Một bên là sự đè nén — sự từ chối để điều gì đó được biết tới, vốn đòi cái giá nơi cơ thể và các mối quan hệ của người làm việc đó. Bên kia là sự cẩu thả — sự lười biếng không chịu mã hoá một cái tên, một món nợ, một lời hứa. Quên có kỉ luật không phải là cả hai.

Các truyền thống tu viện ở Kyoto có một văn khố dài, không uỷ mị, về điều này. Mục đích của một số bài thiền không phải là xoá kí ức mà là nới lỏng cái nắm của nó — để cho phép điều đã xảy ra hiện diện mà không đòi được ôn lại. Có một kỉ luật tương tự, ít mang màu tâm linh hơn, trong cách một bác sĩ cấp cứu giỏi ở São Paulo quyết định không mang ca thứ ba của đêm vào ca thứ tư.

Cứ cho là có những điều không bao giờ được quên, và ta biết là những gì. Các kho lưu trữ ở Berlin và Buenos Aires được xây dựng chính vì lẽ đó: để giữ loại kí ức mà không tâm trí cá nhân nào được yêu cầu phải gánh một mình. Điều tôi bảo vệ không phải là làm loãng kí ức công cộng mà là sự tồn tại, song song với nó, của một kỉ luật riêng tư biết phân biệt giữa điều ta có nghĩa vụ phải nhớ và điều ta được phép, một cách cẩn trọng, buông xuống.

Chính sự khác biệt giữa gửi gắm và xoá bỏ là điều quan trọng. Gửi gắm là gửi nó vào đâu đó — một trang nhật kí, một cuộc trò chuyện với chuyên viên trị liệu hay bạn bè, hồ sơ lưu trữ thiết chế — và biết rằng ta không phải giữ nó trong sự nhớ chủ động.

Chỉ bằng cách phân biệt sự đè nén với sự buông có kỉ luật, ta mới hỏi được câu hỏi đúng dành cho những người ta ngưỡng mộ vì sự điềm tĩnh của họ. Họ điềm tĩnh vì sự kiện đã được gửi gắm đầy đủ, hay vì nó đã bị chôn vùi bằng bạo lực?

Điều gì sẽ mất nếu ta thừa nhận 'quên có chủ ý' vào trong từ vựng đạo đức của đời sống trưởng thành? Rất ít, người ta có thể lập luận, và rất nhiều có thể nhận được. Một vài nỗi bất bình có thể được phép mờ đi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion after a restrictive opener?
options: Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release can we ask the right questions.
Only by distinguishing repression from disciplined release we can ask the right questions.
Only we can ask the right questions by distinguishing repression.
By only distinguishing repression we ask questions can.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Only by + V-ing' triggers inversion of the modal ('can') with the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: Restrictive 'Only by …' moves the modal before the subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'disciplined forgetting consigns; it does not erase'?
options: It deletes memory completely so nothing remains.
It sends memory somewhere rather than pretending it never happened.
It hides memory but keeps it active in the mind.
It both deletes and stores memory at the same time.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines 'consign' as sending the memory elsewhere; 'erase' is pretending it never happened.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option that keeps the consign/erase distinction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition fits a paragraph that concedes before asserting?
options: Therefore, things should not be forgotten and we know which.
Furthermore, no forgetting is allowed under any condition.
Granted, some things should never be forgotten, and we know which. … What I am defending is not the dilution of public memory but the existence, alongside it, of a private discipline …
On the other hand, all forgetting is good in moderation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' concedes the public-memory point first, then narrows the claim — the canonical C1 concede-and-counter.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft most effectively?
options: Precisely the difference matters between consigning and erasing.
What matters is precisely the difference is the consigning and erasing.
The difference, it is precisely matters between consigning.
It is precisely the difference between consigning and erasing that matters here.
mcQuestions.explanation: A is the canonical 'It is X that …' cleft.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the closest paraphrase of 'rehearsing every grief into the next encounter does not honour the past; it just contaminates the present'?
options: Carrying every sorrow forward harms the moment in front of you without serving the past.
Sorrow is not important and should be ignored entirely.
Encounters with sorrow are honourable in every case.
Grief should be performed loudly to be properly remembered.
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps both halves of the original: doesn't honour the past, harms the present.
mcQuestions.hint: Two halves must survive the paraphrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best matches the essay's register?
options: Disciplined forgetting is when you, like, just choose to drop something on purpose.
Disciplined forgetting is the cultivated capacity to receive an event fully, register it, file it, and then withdraw attention from it on purpose.
Forgetting on purpose is something some people just naturally know how to do.
Some forgetting is good and some forgetting is bad, both happen.
mcQuestions.explanation: A nominalises ('cultivated capacity') and lists precise verbs in parallel.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for parallel verbs and a nominalisation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best preserves cohesion when adding a global example?
options: Kyoto monks know about this. São Paulo doctors also.
Monks and doctors do something similar in their work each day.

The monastic traditions of Kyoto have a long, unsentimental literature on this. There is a similar discipline, less spiritualised, in the way a good emergency-room physician in São Paulo decides not to carry the third trauma of the night into the fourth.
Discipline of forgetting happens in Kyoto and also São Paulo, both.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses 'There is a similar discipline … in the way …' as a precise C1 cohesion bridge between examples.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'There is a similar … in …' bridging.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing matches the essay and avoids AI filler?
options: In conclusion, forgetting is one of the most important skills.
Throughout history, humans have always tried to forget.
It is important to note that forgetting requires real practice.
the attention freed by this small monastic discipline could be returned, intact, to the next person standing in front of us — which is, after all, the only person to whom such attention is owed.
mcQuestions.explanation: A closes with a concrete relocation of attention to a person, with em-dashed aside — the controlled landing the essay needs.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'In conclusion', 'Throughout history', 'It is important to note'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses complex concession in C1 register?
options: Granted, some things should never be forgotten, and we know which. … Without that distinction, the demand 'never forget' begins to corrode the very people we are asking to remember.
Granted, things and people and demands and forgetting.
Although forgetting is fine, therefore not forgetting.
Furthermore, never forget always remember always.
mcQuestions.explanation: A concedes the public-memory case, then introduces the corrosive cost of refusing the distinction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is closest to 'a tax that someone will eventually pay'?
options: a tax that the government collects each year
a cost that someone will, in time, have to bear
a payment to be made before forgetting begins
a reward given to those who do not forget
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps the metaphor: a delayed, unavoidable cost.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which item shows the strongest lexical precision for the topic?
options: things, stuff, more stuff
memory and forgetting and things
rumination, repression, release
remembering, very remembering, no remembering
mcQuestions.explanation: Precise, contrastive nouns; standard C1 register for the topic.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three precise contrastive nouns.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence demonstrates a parenthetical aside via em-dashes?
options: Done well and craft and grace, all together.
Outside look grace, inside look craft, done well always.
Grace and craft are well done from outside and inside.
Done well, it has the look, from outside, of grace — and the look, from inside, of a craft.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses an em-dashed aside to extend the metaphor.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the em-dash before a balancing clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which hedging device fits the essay's tone best?
options: Very little, one might argue, and a great deal might be gained.
Absolutely nothing would be lost, that is one hundred percent sure.
Everything will obviously be gained, no doubt at all.
It is definitely the case that nothing will be lost ever.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses 'one might argue' and 'might be' — soft, careful claim-making.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for hedging modals and 'one might argue'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps the discipline-vs-erasure distinction clear?
options: Consigning and erasing are very similar moves in the mind.
To consign something is to send it somewhere; to erase is to pretend it never happened.
Erasing is the same as consigning, mostly.
All forgetting is consigning, never erasing, no matter what.
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps the contrast crisp with a semicolon and parallel infinitives.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the best objection the essay should answer?
options: Why do people forget things at all?
Are archives located in cities or in towns?
How can we tell, in practice, when 'disciplined release' is just repression dressed up?
Should everyone keep a journal forever?
mcQuestions.explanation: A is the essay's central worry — distinguishing genuine release from disguised repression.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the objection the essay itself anticipates.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Name one event from this past month that you have rehearsed more than it deserves. What would 'consigning' it look like for you — a journal entry, a conversation, an archive somewhere outside your own head?
